content.text: Agent frameworks — LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen, CrewAI — ship built-in memory abstractions that manage conversation history and optionally retrieve relevant past context. These modules sit between the application loop and the LLM, injecting recalled history into each prompt. Understanding the differences lets you pick the right module for a task: a sliding-window buffer for short sessions, a summary buffer for long sessions, and a vector-backed retriever for sessions where semantic relevance matters more than position.

ConversationBufferWindowMemory keeps the last k exchange turns (k pairs of human+AI messages) in the context window verbatim. load_memory_variables() returns them under a configurable memory_key that the prompt template splices in. This is the simplest LangChain memory type — zero LLM calls overhead, predictable token usage, and easy to inspect.

# LangChain ConversationBufferWindowMemory — keep the last k turns in context.
# load_memory_variables() injects "history" into the prompt template.

from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferWindowMemory
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.chains import ConversationChain

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

memory = ConversationBufferWindowMemory(
    k=6,                      # keep last 6 exchange turns (12 messages)
    return_messages=True,     # return Message objects, not a flat string
    memory_key="chat_history",
)

chain = ConversationChain(llm=llm, memory=memory, verbose=False)

chain.predict(input="My project deadline is December 15.")
chain.predict(input="The budget cap is $80k.")
reply = chain.predict(input="Summarise the constraints we just set.")
print(reply)

# Inspect what the memory currently holds
stored = memory.load_memory_variables({})["chat_history"]
for msg in stored:
    print(f"[{msg.type}] {msg.content[:80]}")

ConversationSummaryBufferMemory blends the two approaches: recent messages are kept verbatim up to a token budget (max_token_limit), and anything older is compressed into a running summary by the LLM. On each new turn the chain sees both the summary of old context and the verbatim recent turns. This avoids the hard cutoff of a pure sliding window without paying the full cost of re-summarizing everything on each turn.

# ConversationSummaryBufferMemory: keeps recent messages verbatim up to a
# token budget; older messages are compressed into a running summary by the LLM.
# This is the standard choice when the conversation will grow long.

from langchain.memory import ConversationSummaryBufferMemory

summary_memory = ConversationSummaryBufferMemory(
    llm=llm,
    max_token_limit=1200,      # compress when raw history exceeds this
    return_messages=True,
    memory_key="chat_history",
)

chain2 = ConversationChain(llm=llm, memory=summary_memory, verbose=False)

# Simulate a long session
for i in range(20):
    chain2.predict(input=f"Turn {i}: discussing design decision #{i}.")

# After 20 turns, older turns are compressed; only recent ones stay verbatim
state = summary_memory.load_memory_variables({})
print("Summary of old turns:", summary_memory.moving_summary_buffer[:200])
print("Recent messages kept:", len(state["chat_history"]))

VectorStoreRetrieverMemory embeds each conversation turn on save and retrieves the k most semantically similar past turns for each new input. There is no positional window — turns from many sessions ago surface if they are relevant. The trade-off is cost: every turn requires two embedding calls (one on save, one on load) and the retrieved turns may be out of chronological order, which some models find confusing.

# VectorStoreRetrieverMemory: embed each conversation turn and retrieve the
# k most relevant past turns for each new input — pure semantic memory,
# no position-based window.

from langchain.memory import VectorStoreRetrieverMemory
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Qdrant
from langchain_voyageai import VoyageAIEmbeddings
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient

embeddings = VoyageAIEmbeddings(model="voyage-3", voyage_api_key="...")

client = QdrantClient(url="http://localhost:6333")
vectorstore = Qdrant(
    client=client,
    collection_name="langchain_conv",
    embeddings=embeddings,
)

retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 4})
vs_memory = VectorStoreRetrieverMemory(retriever=retriever)

# Save a turn explicitly
vs_memory.save_context(
    inputs={"input": "Alice is the tech lead."},
    outputs={"output": "Noted."},
)

# Later: retrieve relevant past context for a new query
relevant = vs_memory.load_memory_variables({"input": "who leads the team?"})
print(relevant["history"])

LlamaIndex's ChatMemoryBuffer is token-aware: it truncates the oldest messages automatically when the stored history exceeds token_limit, making it safe to use with any LLM without worrying about context overflow. It stores MessageRole-typed objects and integrates directly into SimpleChatEngine and CondensePlusContextChatEngine. The token count is computed by the LLM's tokenizer, so it stays accurate across model swaps.

# LlamaIndex ChatMemoryBuffer: token-aware sliding window that respects the
# model's context limit. The buffer auto-truncates oldest messages when
# token_limit is reached — no explicit k to tune.

from llama_index.core.memory import ChatMemoryBuffer
from llama_index.llms.openai import OpenAI
from llama_index.core.chat_engine import SimpleChatEngine

llm = OpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")

memory = ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(
    token_limit=3000,          # keep at most 3k tokens of history
)

chat_engine = SimpleChatEngine.from_defaults(
    llm=llm,
    memory=memory,
    system_prompt="You are a concise engineering assistant.",
)

r1 = chat_engine.chat("Our API uses REST, not gRPC.")
r2 = chat_engine.chat("What protocol did we decide on?")
print(r2.response)

# Inspect stored messages
for m in memory.get():
    print(f"[{m.role.value}] {m.content[:80]}")

VectorMemory embeds and indexes every stored message, enabling semantic lookup over the full conversation history regardless of length. SimpleCompositeMemory combines a primary ChatMemoryBuffer (for recency) with one or more secondary VectorMemory sources (for relevance). The composite first fills context from the primary buffer, then appends any additional relevant messages retrieved by the secondary sources, de-duplicating if a message already appears in the buffer.

# LlamaIndex VectorMemory: semantic retrieval over all past messages.
# Pair it with ChatMemoryBuffer in a composite to get both recency and relevance.

from llama_index.core.memory import VectorMemory
from llama_index.embeddings.voyageai import VoyageAIEmbedding

embed_model = VoyageAIEmbedding(
    model_name="voyage-3",
    voyage_api_key="...",
)

vector_memory = VectorMemory.from_defaults(
    vector_store=None,      # uses in-process SimpleVectorStore by default
    embed_model=embed_model,
    retriever_kwargs={"similarity_top_k": 3},
)

# Composite: recent buffer (3k tokens) + semantic retrieval (top-3)
from llama_index.core.memory import SimpleCompositeMemory

composite = SimpleCompositeMemory.from_defaults(
    primary_memory=ChatMemoryBuffer.from_defaults(token_limit=3000),
    secondary_memory_sources=[vector_memory],
)

chat_engine2 = SimpleChatEngine.from_defaults(
    llm=llm,
    memory=composite,
)

chat_engine2.chat("Decision: use Postgres 16 for the primary DB.")
chat_engine2.chat("Budget is fixed at $80k.")
reply = chat_engine2.chat("What DB did we choose and what is the budget?")
print(reply.response)
AutoGen: Plugging RAG Memory into ConversableAgent

AutoGen does not ship a built-in semantic memory module, but ConversableAgent is designed to be subclassed. Override generate_reply() to prepend retrieved context from a Chroma or Qdrant collection before delegating to the parent implementation. This pattern works with any vector store that exposes a query interface and requires no changes to the rest of the AutoGen multi-agent graph.
The right memory module depends on session length, retrieval semantics, and cost tolerance.

— LangChain chains expect load_memory_variables() to return a key that matches the prompt template variable. A mismatch silently injects an empty string — the agent 'forgets' everything. Always set memory_key to match the template.
— ChatMemoryBuffer truncates tokens based on its embedder's tokenizer. If you change the LLM without updating the token_limit, the buffer may over-fill the context window.
— LlamaIndex VectorMemory with the default SimpleVectorStore is in-process only. Restart the process and all stored memories vanish. Wire it to Qdrant or Chroma for persistence.
— The LLM that compresses old turns may hallucinate or omit details. Periodically log summary_memory.moving_summary_buffer in staging and compare it against the raw history.

— Using both a LangChain memory object and a separate Qdrant collection for the same session creates two sources of truth that diverge. Pick one authoritative store and have the framework module point at it.

CrewAI ships four memory types activated through the Crew constructor: short-term (sliding window over task outputs), long-term (vector store persisted across runs), entity (named entity extraction and tracking), and contextual (assembled from the other three for each task). Long-term memory requires a RAGStorage backend — configure it with a provider (Qdrant, Chroma, Pinecone) and an embedder. Short-term and entity memory use in-process stores and reset when the process exits.

# CrewAI ships built-in short-term, long-term, entity, and contextual memory.
# Enable them in the Crew constructor — no manual embedding calls required.

from crewai import Crew, Agent, Task, Process
from crewai.memory import (
    ShortTermMemory,
    LongTermMemory,
    EntityMemory,
)
from crewai.memory.storage.rag_storage import RAGStorage

# Configure a shared Qdrant-backed long-term store
lt_storage = RAGStorage(
    type='long_term',
    embedder_config={
        'provider': 'voyageai',
        'config': {'model': 'voyage-3', 'api_key': '...'},
    },
    storage_config={
        'provider': 'qdrant',
        'config': {'url': 'http://localhost:6333', 'collection_name': 'crew_lt'},
    },
)

researcher = Agent(
    role='Research Analyst',
    goal='Find and summarise technical information.',
    backstory='Expert in technical research.',
    memory=True,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher],
    tasks=[Task(description='Analyse FastAPI performance characteristics.', agent=researcher)],
    process=Process.sequential,
    # Memory flags — each type is independent
    memory=True,                        # enables short-term + contextual
    long_term_memory=LongTermMemory(storage=lt_storage),
    entity_memory=EntityMemory(),       # tracks named entities across tasks
    verbose=False,
)

result = crew.kickoff()
# Short-term memory auto-resets between runs.
# Long-term and entity memory persist in the Qdrant collection.

LangChain's default in-memory store is discarded when the process exits. Replace it with a persistent backend by passing a chat_memory argument. RedisChatMessageHistory writes every turn to Redis using the session_id as the key. Any process that constructs a ConversationBufferWindowMemory with the same session_id and Redis URL picks up the stored history automatically — no serialisation or loading code required.

# Persisting LangChain memory to Redis so it survives process restarts
# and scales across multiple worker processes.

from langchain_community.chat_message_histories import RedisChatMessageHistory
from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferWindowMemory
from langchain.chains import ConversationChain
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI

SESSION_ID = 'user_42_session_7'

# RedisChatMessageHistory persists each turn to Redis automatically
redis_history = RedisChatMessageHistory(
    session_id=SESSION_ID,
    url='redis://localhost:6379/0',
    ttl=86400,   # expire session after 24 hours of inactivity
)

memory = ConversationBufferWindowMemory(
    chat_memory=redis_history,
    k=8,
    return_messages=True,
    memory_key='chat_history',
)

llm = ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4o-mini', temperature=0)
chain = ConversationChain(llm=llm, memory=memory)

# Turns are written to Redis; restoring a session is just re-creating
# RedisChatMessageHistory with the same session_id.
reply = chain.predict(input='What framework are we using?')
print(reply)

# Inspect all stored messages for this session
for m in redis_history.messages:
    print(f'[{m.type}] {m.content[:80]}')

# Clear this session's history
# redis_history.clear()
LangChain ships three graduated memory types: buffer window (recency), summary buffer (recency + compression), and vector store retriever (semantic relevance).
LlamaIndex ChatMemoryBuffer is token-aware and pairs with VectorMemory in a SimpleCompositeMemory for combined recency + relevance.
AutoGen does not bundle a semantic memory module but is designed for subclassing — override generate_reply() to prepend retrieved context from any vector store.
Match the module to the session profile: buffer for short sessions, summary buffer for long coherent sessions, vector-backed retriever for cross-session recall.
Key correctness checks: memory_key matches the prompt template, token_limit is set correctly for the target LLM, and the vector store backend persists between process restarts.
